title: License Plate Capture Range Guide
summary: Understand how resolution, FOV, plate target pixels, plate width, and distance affect license-plate detail.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates whether a camera view provides enough pixels across a license plate at the entered distance and target requirement.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning gate, lane, driveway, or parking-area views where plate detail matters more than general scene coverage.
bullets: Early design planning.
Camera placement review.
Expansion or retrofit validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical physical security planning terms.

items.label: Horizontal Resolution (px)
items.description: The number of horizontal pixels in the camera image.
items.whyItMatters: Resolution sets the total pixel budget available across the lane or scene width.
items.commonMistake: Assuming higher resolution fixes plate capture without checking angle, distance, and shutter/lighting.

items.label: Horizontal FOV (deg)
items.description: The side-to-side lens angle for the plate view.
items.whyItMatters: Plate capture often needs a narrower view than general surveillance so more pixels land on the plate.
items.commonMistake: Using an overview camera for plate detail and expecting readable plates.

items.label: Pixels per Plate Target
items.description: The desired number of pixels across the license plate.
items.whyItMatters: This target defines whether the calculated plate detail is likely sufficient for the intended review task.
items.commonMistake: Choosing a pixel target without accounting for angle, motion, glare, and nighttime performance.

items.label: Plate Width (ft)
items.description: The assumed physical width of the license plate target.
items.whyItMatters: Plate width converts scene pixel density into pixels across the actual plate.
items.commonMistake: Using lane width or vehicle width instead of the plate width.

items.label: Distance (ft)
items.description: The distance from the camera to the plate capture point.
items.whyItMatters: Distance strongly affects plate detail and may require a narrower lens or closer camera placement.
items.commonMistake: Measuring to the nearest vehicle location instead of the actual capture zone.

links.label: Validate view width and density
links.note: Pixel Density helps confirm the scene-wide detail behind the plate-specific check.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the entered view likely meets the plate pixel target with reasonable margin. Watch means angle, light, shutter, speed, and camera settings should be reviewed. Risk means the view is likely too wide, too far, or too low-detail for reliable plate capture.
bullets: Plate capture is sensitive to angle, headlight glare, IR reflection, shutter speed, and vehicle motion.
A successful plate view is usually tighter than a general overview camera.
Validate with real vehicle passes under day and night conditions.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most physical security planning problems come from treating camera coverage, recognition detail, mounting position, and lighting as separate decisions. In the field, those choices interact: a wider view lowers pixel density, a higher mount changes target geometry, and poor lighting can erase detail that looked acceptable on paper.
bullets: Sizing a camera view only by how wide the scene looks, without checking target detail.
Using clean daytime assumptions for a scene that must also work at night, in glare, or under motion.
Ignoring overlap, obstructions, mounting limits, and the actual target plane.
Treating calculated values as final design approval instead of documented planning assumptions.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: These guides explain the planning logic behind the tool. They do not replace manufacturer documentation, site survey, camera testing, client acceptance criteria, code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Validate against the actual camera model, lens, sensor, encoder settings, and mounting location.
Confirm lighting, reflections, obstructions, weather, and target movement before relying on a design.
Use calculated outputs as design evidence and review notes, not as a substitute for field validation.
Leave practical margin when the scene is important, difficult, or expected to change.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, on-site testing, legal review, privacy review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Document assumptions before implementation.
Review the design with the owner, integrator, manufacturer, and applicable authority when required.
Test representative views before treating a camera placement as final.
